pairs.question: What was the Islami Jamhuri Ittihad (IJI) and when did it exist?
pairs.answer: The Islami Jamhuri Ittihad (IJI), also known as the Islamic Democratic Alliance (IDA), was a right-wing conservative political alliance in Pakistan that existed between 1988 and 1993. It was formed to oppose the Pakistan People's Party (PPP) led by Benazir Bhutto.

pairs.question: Which major parties were part of the Islami Jamhuri Ittihad (IJI)?
pairs.answer: The major components of the IJI included the Pakistan Muslim League (PML), which accounted for 80% of its electoral candidates, the National Peoples Party (NPP), and Jamaat-e-Islami (JI).

pairs.question: What role did the ISI play in the formation of the IJI?
pairs.answer: The Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) agency, under director Hamid Gul, played a significant role in forming the right-of-centre political alliance. This was part of broader efforts to consolidate anti-PPP forces.

pairs.question: Who was the head of the IJI and who was its most prominent leader?
pairs.answer: Ghulam Mustafa Jatoi was the head of the IJI, but Nawaz Sharif, a young industrialist appointed as Chief Minister of Punjab by Zia ul-Haq, became its most resourceful leader.

pairs.question: How did Nawaz Sharif gain prominence within the IJI?
pairs.answer: Nawaz Sharif emerged as a powerful politician after the 1988 elections, winning 53 seats in the National Assembly. His leadership in Punjab, where he formed an IJI administration in December 1988, strengthened his political position and eventually led to his becoming Prime Minister in 1990.

pairs.question: What was the election outcome for the IJI in 1990 compared to the PPP?
pairs.answer: In the 1990 elections, the IJI received a strong mandate, winning 105 seats compared to 45 seats for the Pakistan Democratic Alliance (PDA), of which the PPP was the main component. Opposition groups alleged large-scale selective rigging in the elections.

pairs.question: Why did the IJI coalition dissolve before the 1993 elections?
pairs.answer: The IJI coalition no longer existed in 1993 because religious parties focused more on forming a viable electoral alliance rather than supporting Nawaz Sharif, who was the only figure capable of challenging Benazir Bhutto.
